        tool_numbers.update(extract_numbers(output))

    # Extract numbers from the agent's response
    response_numbers = extract_numbers(str(outputs))

    if not response_numbers:
        return Feedback(value=True, rationale="No numbers in response to verify.")

    # Check how many response numbers are grounded in tool output
    grounded = response_numbers & tool_numbers

    if not grounded:
        return Feedback(
            value=False,
            rationale="No response numbers found in tool output - possible hallucination.",
        )

    return Feedback(
        value=True,
        rationale=f"{len(grounded)} of {len(response_numbers)} response numbers grounded in tool output.",
    )


@scorer
def scope_guard(inputs, outputs, expectations, trace):
    """Off-topic questions should get zero tool calls and a polite decline."""
    expected_tools = expectations.get("expected_tools", [])

    # This scorer only applies to off-topic scenarios (expected_tools is empty)
    if expected_tools:
        return Feedback(value=True, rationale="Not an off-topic scenario, skipping.")

    actual_tools = get_tool_calls(trace)

    # Check 1: No tools should be called
    if actual_tools:
        return Feedback(
            value=False,
            rationale=f"Called tools for off-topic question: {actual_tools}",
        )

    # Check 2: Response should contain decline language
    response_lower = str(outputs).lower()
    decline_phrases = [
        "can only",
        "only assist",
        "only help",
        "sales",
        "cannot help",
        "not able to",
        "outside",
        "beyond my",
        "don't have",
        "do not have",
    ]

    if any(phrase in response_lower for phrase in decline_phrases):
        return Feedback(value=True, rationale="Correctly declined off-topic question.")

    return Feedback(
        value=False,
        rationale=(
            f"No decline language found in response. "
            f"Expected phrases like 'can only assist with sales'. "
            f"Got: {str(outputs)[:200]}"
        ),
    )


@scorer
def efficiency(inputs, outputs, expectations, trace):
    """Check the agent did not loop or make excessive calls."""
    actual_tools = get_tool_calls(trace)
    max_calls = expectations.get("max_tool_calls", 4)

    # Check total tool calls are not excessive
    if len(actual_tools) > max_calls:
        return Feedback(
            value=False,
            rationale=(
                f"Too many tool calls: {len(actual_tools)} "
                f"(expected {max_calls} or fewer)"
            ),
        )

    return Feedback(
        value=True,
        rationale=f"Efficient: {len(actual_tools)} tool call(s) within limit of {max_calls}.",
    )

Did you know? MLflow provides a built-in ToolCallEfficiency scorer that checks for redundant tool calls using an LLM judge. Our custom efficiency scorer above is deterministic (no LLM call, free, instant), which makes it better for CI. But if you want a quick start without writing custom code, ToolCallEfficiency() from mlflow.genai.scorers works out of the box.

Now update eval/evaluate.py (path from project root) with all four scorers. Same setup - only the import and scorers list change:

"""Run evaluation suite for the sales agent."""

import os
os.environ["MLFLOW_ENABLED"] = "false"

import mlflow
from uuid import uuid4

from first_react_agent.config import AICoreConfig
from first_react_agent.agent import create_app
from eval.scorers import tool_selection, groundedness, scope_guard, efficiency  # added efficiency
from eval.dataset import SALES_AGENT_DATASET

# Setup (same as Step 4 - see explanation there)
config = AICoreConfig()
mlflow.set_tracking_uri(config.mlflow_tracking_uri)
mlflow.set_experiment(config.mlflow_experiment_name)

agent = create_app()

mlflow.langchain.autolog(log_traces=True, run_tracer_inline=True)
mlflow.langchain.autolog(disable=True)


def predict_fn(question: str) -> str:
    """Run the agent and return the final response text."""
    result = agent.invoke(
        {"messages": [{"role": "user", "content": question}]},
        {"configurable": {"thread_id": str(uuid4())}},
    )
    return result["messages"][-1].content


results = mlflow.genai.evaluate(
    data=SALES_AGENT_DATASET,
    predict_fn=predict_fn,
    scorers=[tool_selection, groundedness, scope_guard, efficiency],  # added efficiency
)

print(results.tables["eval_results"])

Run from the project root (first-react-agent/) :

uv run python -m eval.evaluate

When it finishes, copy the URL from the terminal output and open it in your browser. You will see two panels:

Left panel: Run summary. Each row is a complete evaluation run. The columns show the aggregate score for each scorer - 1.0 means every scenario passed. Your latest run should show 1.0 for all four scorers: tool_selection, scope_guard, groundedness, and efficiency.

Right panel: Trace-level detail. Click a run to expand it. Each row is one trace. You will see evaluation traces (one per scenario) and duplicate traces from autolog (harmless noise). The evaluation traces show:

  • Request and Response - the question and the agent's answer
  • Tokens and Execution time - cost and latency per scenario
  • Assessments - scorer results (True for evaluation traces, null for autolog duplicates)
  • Expectations - expected_tools and max_tool_calls from the dataset, so you can see at a glance what each scenario expected

The summary chart at the top right shows the overall pass rate across all evaluation traces. Click any evaluation trace row to inspect the full agent execution - every LLM call, tool call, input, and output.

This is your golden trajectory suite. Run it whenever you change the agent - system prompt, tools, model, or configuration. If a scenario that used to pass starts failing, you have found a regression.

In a real deployment workflow, this suite becomes your quality gate:

  • Before deploying - run the full suite. All scenarios must pass. If any fail, fix the agent before shipping.
  • Nightly in production - run the same suite on a schedule. If a model update or infrastructure change breaks a behavior, you catch it within 24 hours instead of waiting for user complaints.
  • After every code change - add the deterministic scorers to your CI pipeline. They run fast (no LLM judge calls) and cost nothing.

Layer 4: Subjective Quality - Some things cannot be checked with code. Step 8 introduces LLM-as-Judge for the genuinely subjective dimensions of response quality.

Step 8: LLM-as-Judge - When Code Cannot Decide

Theory

Steps 4-7 covered deterministic scorers - pure Python checks with definitive pass/fail answers. But some quality dimensions are subjective:

  • "Was the response clear and easy to understand?"
  • "Did it fully answer the question, or leave things out?"
  • "Is the response well-structured for a business user?"

You cannot write a Python function to reliably answer these. That is where LLM-as-Judge comes in: you use an LLM to evaluate the agent's output against natural-language criteria.

MLflow provides a built-in scorer called Guidelines for this. From the MLflow Guidelines docs: a Guidelines scorer evaluates LLM outputs against natural-language rules you define. It returns a binary pass/fail verdict with a detailed rationale. It supports 14+ model providers out of the box (OpenAI, Anthropic, Bedrock, Gemini, and more) via the model parameter.

Limitation with SAP AI Core: MLflow's built-in judges require a direct provider API key (e.g., OPENAI_API_KEY for OpenAI, ANTHROPIC_API_KEY for Anthropic). SAP AI Core's gen_ai_hub proxy authenticates via OAuth2 client credentials, which is not directly compatible with MLflow's judge model system. You could install LiteLLM to bridge this, but we will keep our dependencies minimal.

Instead, we will write a custom LLM judge scorer using the same create_llm() from client.py that our agent already uses. This reuses our SAP AI Core setup with zero additional configuration or API keys. It also shows you exactly how LLM-as-Judge works under the hood - the same pattern that Guidelines implements internally.

When to use deterministic vs LLM-as-Judge:

CheckMethodWhy
Right tool was calledDeterministicCompare names - no judgment needed
Numbers match tool outputDeterministicExact comparison
Declined off-topic questionDeterministicCheck for phrases + zero tool calls
Response is clear and well-structuredLLM-as-JudgeSubjective, requires understanding
Response fully answers the questionLLM-as-JudgeRequires judgment on completeness

Rule of thumb: if you can write a Python function to check it, do not use an LLM judge. LLM judges are more expensive (they make an LLM call per evaluation), slower, and non-deterministic (may give different results on re-runs). Reserve them for genuinely subjective dimensions.

Where LLM judges fit in your workflow: Deterministic scorers (Steps 4-7) are your daily checks - fast, free, reliable. LLM judges are your weekly or pre-release checks - they catch subtle quality issues that code cannot detect, like confusing phrasing or incomplete answers. In production, run deterministic scorers in CI on every commit, and run LLM judges on a nightly or weekly schedule to track response quality over time.

MLflow also provides other predefined judges beyond Guidelines. Here is the full list:

Response quality judges (work on inputs/outputs, no traces needed) :

JudgeWhat it checks Needs ground truth?
RelevanceToQueryDoes the response address the user's input?No
CorrectnessAre the expected facts supported by the response?Yes
CompletenessDoes the response address all parts of the question?No
FluencyIs the response grammatically correct and natural?No
SafetyDoes the response avoid harmful content?No
EquivalenceDoes the response match the expected output?Yes
SummarizationIs the summary faithful and comprehensive?No
ExpectationsGuidelinesDoes the response meet per-row guidelines?Yes (per-row)

ExpectationsGuidelines is a variant of Guidelines that reads its rules from the expectations field in each data row, so different test cases can have different criteria.

Tool call judges (require traces with dedicated TOOL spans - not all LLM providers create these; see the note in Step 4 about why we parse messages instead) :

JudgeWhat it checksNeeds ground truth?
ToolCallCorrectnessWere the right tools called with correct arguments?No
ToolCallEfficiencyWere there redundant or duplicate tool calls?No

RAG judges (require traces with retrieval spans) :

JudgeWhat it checksNeeds ground truth?
RetrievalGroundednessIs the response grounded in retrieved documents?No
RetrievalRelevanceAre the retrieved documents relevant to the query?No
RetrievalSufficiencyDo the retrieved documents contain enough info to answer?Yes

All predefined judges are imported from mlflow.genai.scorers.

For our sales agent, we will write custom LLM judge scorers that call the same SAP AI Core model the agent uses. If you have a direct provider API key (e.g., OpenAI), you could use Guidelines instead - the pattern is the same, just less code.

Lab

Update eval/scorers.py (path from project root). Here is the complete file with all six scorers - the four deterministic scorers from Step 7 plus two new LLM judge scorers:

"""Evaluation scorers for the sales agent."""

import re

from mlflow.entities import Feedback
from mlflow.genai.scorers import scorer


def extract_numbers(text: str) -> set[str]:
    """Extract all numbers from text, including decimals and comma-separated."""
    numbers = set()
    text = str(text)
    # Pass 1: treat commas as thousand separators (e.g., "$1,799.46")
    for match in re.findall(r"\d[\d,]*\.\d+|\d[\d,]*", text):
        numbers.add(match.replace(",", ""))
    # Pass 2: split by commas first, then extract (handles CSV like "4,129.99")
    for field in text.replace("\n", ",").split(","):
        for match in re.findall(r"\d+\.\d+|\d+", field.strip()):
            numbers.add(match)
    return numbers


def get_tool_calls(trace):
    """Extract tool names from assistant messages in the trace."""
    tools = []
    for span in trace.search_spans():
        if hasattr(span, "inputs") and isinstance(span.inputs, dict):
            for msg in span.inputs.get("messages", []):
                if msg.get("role") == "assistant":
                    for tc in msg.get("tool_calls", []):
                        name = tc.get("function", {}).get("name")
                        if name:
                            tools.append(name)
    return tools


def get_tool_outputs(trace):
    """Extract tool output text from tool messages in the trace."""
    outputs = []
    for span in trace.search_spans():
        if hasattr(span, "inputs") and isinstance(span.inputs, dict):
            for msg in span.inputs.get("messages", []):
                if msg.get("role") == "tool":
                    outputs.append(msg.get("content", ""))
    return outputs


@scorer
def tool_selection(inputs, outputs, expectations, trace):
    """Check that the agent called the expected tools."""
    actual_tools = get_tool_calls(trace)
    expected_tools = expectations.get("expected_tools", [])

    # If no tools expected (e.g., off-topic question), check none were called
    if not expected_tools:
        if not actual_tools:
            return Feedback(value=True, rationale="Correctly called no tools.")
        return Feedback(
            value=False,
            rationale=f"Expected no tool calls, but called: {actual_tools}",
        )

    # Check each expected tool was called
    missing = [t for t in expected_tools if t not in actual_tools]
    if missing:
        return Feedback(
            value=False,
            rationale=f"Missing tools: {missing}. Called: {actual_tools}",
        )

    return Feedback(value=True, rationale=f"All expected tools called: {actual_tools}")


@scorer
def groundedness(inputs, outputs, trace):
    """Response numbers should come from tool output, not be hallucinated."""
    tool_outputs = get_tool_outputs(trace)

    if not tool_outputs:
        # No tools called - nothing to ground against
        return Feedback(value=True, rationale="No tool calls to check against.")

    # Collect all numbers from tool outputs
    tool_numbers = set()
    for output in tool_outputs:
        tool_numbers.update(extract_numbers(output))

    # Extract numbers from the agent's response
    response_numbers = extract_numbers(str(outputs))

    if not response_numbers:
        return Feedback(value=True, rationale="No numbers in response to verify.")

    # Check how many response numbers are grounded in tool output
    grounded = response_numbers & tool_numbers

    if not grounded:
        return Feedback(
            value=False,
            rationale="No response numbers found in tool output - possible hallucination.",
        )

    return Feedback(
        value=True,
        rationale=f"{len(grounded)} of {len(response_numbers)} response numbers grounded in tool output.",
    )


@scorer
def scope_guard(inputs, outputs, expectations, trace):
    """Off-topic questions should get zero tool calls and a polite decline."""
    expected_tools = expectations.get("expected_tools", [])

    # This scorer only applies to off-topic scenarios (expected_tools is empty)
    if expected_tools:
        return Feedback(value=True, rationale="Not an off-topic scenario, skipping.")

    actual_tools = get_tool_calls(trace)

    # Check 1: No tools should be called
    if actual_tools:
        return Feedback(
            value=False,
            rationale=f"Called tools for off-topic question: {actual_tools}",
        )

    # Check 2: Response should contain decline language
    response_lower = str(outputs).lower()
    decline_phrases = [
        "can only",
        "only assist",
        "only help",
        "sales",
        "cannot help",
        "not able to",
        "outside",
        "beyond my",
        "don't have",
        "do not have",
    ]

    if any(phrase in response_lower for phrase in decline_phrases):
        return Feedback(value=True, rationale="Correctly declined off-topic question.")

    return Feedback(
        value=False,
        rationale=(
            f"No decline language found in response. "
            f"Expected phrases like 'can only assist with sales'. "
            f"Got: {str(outputs)[:200]}"
        ),
    )


@scorer
def efficiency(inputs, outputs, expectations, trace):
    """Check the agent did not loop or make excessive calls."""
    actual_tools = get_tool_calls(trace)
    max_calls = expectations.get("max_tool_calls", 4)

    # Check total tool calls are not excessive
    if len(actual_tools) > max_calls:
        return Feedback(
            value=False,
            rationale=(
                f"Too many tool calls: {len(actual_tools)} "
                f"(expected {max_calls} or fewer)"
            ),
        )

    return Feedback(
        value=True,
        rationale=f"Efficient: {len(actual_tools)} tool call(s) within limit of {max_calls}.",
    )


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# LLM-as-Judge scorers (use the same SAP AI Core model as the agent)
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------

from first_react_agent.client import create_llm
from first_react_agent.config import AICoreConfig

# Create a separate LLM instance for judging
_judge_config = AICoreConfig()
_judge_llm = create_llm(_judge_config)


def _llm_judge(question: str, response: str, guidelines: list[str]) -> Feedback:
    """Call the LLM to judge a response against guidelines."""
    rules = "\n".join(f"- {g}" for g in guidelines)
    prompt = (
        "You are an evaluation judge for a sales data assistant. "
        "The agent can ONLY answer questions about sales data. "
        "For any off-topic question, the agent is expected to politely decline and explain what it can help with. "
        "A polite decline for an off-topic question is CORRECT behavior.\n\n"
        "Determine if the response meets all applicable guidelines.\n\n"
        f"Guidelines:\n{rules}\n\n"
        f"User question: {question}\n"
        f"Agent response: {response}\n\n"
        "Answer YES if the response meets all applicable guidelines, or NO if it does not. "
        "Then explain your reasoning in one sentence.\n\n"
        "Format: YES/NO: <rationale>"
    )
    result = _judge_llm.invoke(prompt)
    answer = result.content.strip()
    passed = answer.upper().startswith("YES")
    return Feedback(value=passed, rationale=answer)


@scorer
def clarity(inputs, outputs):
    """LLM judge: is the response clear and well-structured?"""
    return _llm_judge(
        question=inputs["question"],
        response=str(outputs),
        guidelines=[
            "The response should be well-structured and easy to read.",
            "If the response includes numbers, they should be clearly presented, not buried in long paragraphs.",
            "If the agent declines a question, the decline should be polite and explain what it can help with.",
        ],
    )


@scorer
def completeness(inputs, outputs):
    """LLM judge: does the response fully address the question?"""
    return _llm_judge(
        question=inputs["question"],
        response=str(outputs),
        guidelines=[
            "The response should directly address the user's question.",
            "If the user asks about sales data, the response should include specific numbers, not vague statements.",
            "If the question is outside the agent's scope, the response should clearly explain what it can help with instead.",
        ],
    )

The pattern is straightforward: _llm_judge is a helper that formats a prompt with your guidelines, sends it to the LLM, and parses YES/NO from the response. Each scorer defines its own guidelines and delegates to this helper. This is exactly what MLflow's Guidelines does internally - we are just doing it explicitly with our own LLM.

The dataset (eval/dataset.py) stays the same - the LLM judges work with the existing test cases.

Now update eval/evaluate.py (path from project root). Same setup as before - the only change from Step 7 is the two new imports and adding them to the scorers list:

"""Run evaluation suite for the sales agent."""

import os
os.environ["MLFLOW_ENABLED"] = "false"

import mlflow
from uuid import uuid4

from first_react_agent.config import AICoreConfig
from first_react_agent.agent import create_app
from eval.scorers import tool_selection, groundedness, scope_guard, efficiency
from eval.scorers import clarity, completeness  # LLM judge scorers
from eval.dataset import SALES_AGENT_DATASET

# Setup (same as Step 4 - see explanation there)
config = AICoreConfig()
mlflow.set_tracking_uri(config.mlflow_tracking_uri)
mlflow.set_experiment(config.mlflow_experiment_name)

agent = create_app()

mlflow.langchain.autolog(log_traces=True, run_tracer_inline=True)
mlflow.langchain.autolog(disable=True)


def predict_fn(question: str) -> str:
    """Run the agent and return the final response text."""
    result = agent.invoke(
        {"messages": [{"role": "user", "content": question}]},
        {"configurable": {"thread_id": str(uuid4())}},
    )
    return result["messages"][-1].content


results = mlflow.genai.evaluate(
    data=SALES_AGENT_DATASET,
    predict_fn=predict_fn,
    scorers=[
        # Deterministic (fast, reliable, free)
        tool_selection,
        groundedness,
        scope_guard,
        efficiency,
        # LLM-as-Judge (slower, costs tokens, subjective)
        clarity,
        completeness,
    ],
)

print(results.tables["eval_results"])

The only change from Step 7's evaluate.py is the two new imports (clarity, completeness) and adding them to the scorers list. The judge scorers live in scorers.py alongside the deterministic ones - same file, same pattern.

Run the evaluation:

From the project root (first-react-agent/) :

uv run python -m eval.evaluate

When it finishes, copy the URL from the terminal output and open it in your browser.

The evaluation now takes longer because each test case makes two additional LLM calls (one for clarity, one for completeness). In the MLflow UI you will see the assessment columns for all scorers under Assessments - use the Columns dropdown to toggle them. You will also see the expectations columns (expected_tools, max_tool_calls) under Expectations.

The evaluation traces show assessment results for all scorers, while the autolog duplicate traces show null (these are harmless noise from the agent's internal tracing).

The summary chart shows all scorers at 100% - every scenario passes all LLM judges. The off-topic declines ("Write me a poem about cats", "What's the weather in Berlin?") correctly pass because our judge prompt tells the LLM that polite declines are expected behavior for a sales-only agent.

Notice the key difference between the two scorer types:

  • Deterministic scorers (tool_selection, groundedness, scope_guard, efficiency) run instantly and give the same result every time
  • LLM judges (clarity, completeness) take a few seconds each (they make LLM calls) and may vary slightly across runs - a response that gets "True" on one run might get "False" on the next

This is why we recommend the deterministic-first approach. Use code-based checks for everything you can, and reserve LLM judges for the genuinely subjective dimensions. If you see an LLM judge fail, click the row to inspect the rationale before treating it as a real issue.

Step 9: Anti-Patterns - What NOT to Do

Theory

Before we wrap up, let's cover the common mistakes people make when evaluating agents. These will save you hours of debugging:

1. Do not assert exact response strings

# BAD - will fail on every re-run because LLM wording varies
assert response == "January 2025 had 4 orders with total revenue of $1,159.62"

# GOOD - assert the facts, not the words
assert "4" in response          # order count
assert "1159.62" in response    # revenue (or use groundedness scorer)

LLM responses vary on every run. The wording changes, the structure changes, but the facts should be consistent. Assert facts, not strings.

2. Do not use LLM-as-Judge for everything

# BAD - using an LLM judge to check if a tool was called
@scorer
def tool_check(inputs, outputs):
    return _llm_judge(inputs["question"], str(outputs),
        ["The agent should have called get_monthly_sales"])

# GOOD - deterministic check (faster, cheaper, reliable)
@scorer
def tool_check(trace):
    tools = get_tool_calls(trace)
    return "get_monthly_sales" in tools

LLM judges cost money, take time, and give non-deterministic results. If you can write a Python function for it, do that instead.

3. Do not mock the LLM

# BAD - proves nothing about real model behavior
mock_llm.return_value = "January had 4 orders"
response = agent.invoke(question)
assert "4 orders" in response  # Of course it passes - you mocked it

# GOOD - use the real LLM
response = agent.invoke(question)  # Real LLM on SAP AI Core
assert_grounded(response, trace)   # Check real behavior

Mocking the LLM tests your code, not your agent. Use mocks for unit tests (Layer 1). Use real LLM calls for behavioral evaluation (Layers 2-4).

4. Do not skip evaluation because "the LLM is good enough"
Model behavior drifts across versions. What works today may break tomorrow when the model updates. Nightly regression runs (your golden trajectories) catch drift before users notice.

5. Do not run LLM judges in CI
LLM judges are slow (2-5 seconds per evaluation), non-deterministic (different results each run), and expensive. They make CI flaky. Instead:

  • CI - run deterministic scorers only (tool_selection, groundedness, scope_guard, efficiency). Fast, reliable, free.
  • Nightly - run the full suite including LLM judges. Compare scores over time.

6. Do not apply RAG metrics to tool-calling agents
MLflow provides RAG-specific scorers like RetrievalGroundedness, RetrievalRelevance, and RetrievalSufficiency (see predefined judges). These are designed for retrieval-augmented generation - agents that search documents. Your sales agent calls structured tools (functions that query data), not document retrievers. Use tool-calling metrics instead: tool selection accuracy, parameter correctness, efficiency.

7. Know what is built-in before writing custom scorers
MLflow provides built-in tool call scorers: ToolCallCorrectness and ToolCallEfficiency. These are LLM-based judges that analyze traces for correct tool usage and redundant calls - exactly what our custom tool_selection and efficiency scorers do.

# These built-in scorers exist - check before writing your own
from mlflow.genai.scorers import ToolCallCorrectness, ToolCallEfficiency

We wrote custom scorers in this blog because (a) it teaches you how scorers work, and (b) our custom versions are deterministic (no LLM call, free, fast). But if you need a quick start and do not mind using LLM judge tokens, the built-in ones work out of the box.

Putting it all together - evaluation in a real deployment pipeline:

Now that you know the anti-patterns, here is the recommended approach for evaluating agents before and during production:

 

StageWhat to runHow often Why
Local developmentFull suite (deterministic + LLM judges)Every time you change the agentCatch issues before they leave your machine
CI pipelineDeterministic scorers onlyEvery commit / pull requestFast, free, reliable. Blocks bad code from merging
Pre-deploy gateFull suite (deterministic + LLM judges)Before every deploymentFinal quality check. No green suite, no deploy
Production (nightly)Full suite against production endpointEvery nightCatch model drift, infrastructure changes, provider updates
Production (monitoring)Lightweight deterministic checks on sampled trafficContinuousSpot regressions in real user interactions

The scorers you built in Steps 4-8 are reusable across all these stages. The only thing that changes is which scorers you run and how often.

Step 10: The Complete Evaluation Script

Theory

Let's organize everything into a clean, production-ready project structure. Good evaluation code follows the same principles as good application code: separation of concerns, clear naming, easy to run.

Why separate files matter: When your evaluation lives in a single script, it is hard to reuse and hard to maintain. By splitting scorers, datasets, and the runner into separate files, you can:

  • Reuse the same scorers across different agents - just change the dataset
  • Add new test scenarios without touching the runner or scorer code
  • Run different scorer combinations for different purposes (CI vs nightly vs pre-deploy)
  • Version your datasets in git and track how expectations evolve over time

The pattern:

eval/
├── __init__.py
├── scorers.py      # All scorer definitions (deterministic + LLM judges)
├── dataset.py      # Golden trajectory definitions
└── evaluate.py     # Main runner - ties scorers + dataset + agent together

Scorers are reusable. You can write a tool_selection scorer once and use it across different agents - just change the expected_tools in the dataset.

Datasets are versioned. As your agent evolves, you add new scenarios and update expectations. Keep old scenarios to catch regressions.

The runner is the entry point. It disables the agent's own tracing (so evaluate() can manage it), configures the MLflow server, creates the agent, configures LangChain's tracer for evaluate()'s thread pool, defines the prediction function, and calls mlflow.genai.evaluate(). All of this was explained in Step 4 - the setup is the same in every evaluate.py file.

Lab

Here is the complete code for all three files. If you have been following along, these are the final versions - replace the contents of each file entirely.

eval/scorers.py (path from project root) - all scorers in one place (deterministic + LLM judges) :

"""Evaluation scorers for the sales agent."""

import re

from mlflow.entities import Feedback
from mlflow.genai.scorers import scorer


def extract_numbers(text: str) -> set[str]:
    """Extract all numbers from text, including decimals and comma-separated."""
    numbers = set()
    text = str(text)
    # Pass 1: treat commas as thousand separators (e.g., "$1,799.46")
    for match in re.findall(r"\d[\d,]*\.\d+|\d[\d,]*", text):
        numbers.add(match.replace(",", ""))
    # Pass 2: split by commas first, then extract (handles CSV like "4,129.99")
    for field in text.replace("\n", ",").split(","):
        for match in re.findall(r"\d+\.\d+|\d+", field.strip()):
            numbers.add(match)
    return numbers


def get_tool_calls(trace):
    """Extract tool names from assistant messages in the trace."""
    tools = []
    for span in trace.search_spans():
        if hasattr(span, "inputs") and isinstance(span.inputs, dict):
            for msg in span.inputs.get("messages", []):
                if msg.get("role") == "assistant":
                    for tc in msg.get("tool_calls", []):
                        name = tc.get("function", {}).get("name")
                        if name:
                            tools.append(name)
    return tools


def get_tool_outputs(trace):
    """Extract tool output text from tool messages in the trace."""
    outputs = []
    for span in trace.search_spans():
        if hasattr(span, "inputs") and isinstance(span.inputs, dict):
            for msg in span.inputs.get("messages", []):
                if msg.get("role") == "tool":
                    outputs.append(msg.get("content", ""))
    return outputs


@scorer
def tool_selection(inputs, outputs, expectations, trace):
    """Check that the agent called the expected tools."""
    actual_tools = get_tool_calls(trace)
    expected_tools = expectations.get("expected_tools", [])

    # If no tools expected (e.g., off-topic question), check none were called
    if not expected_tools:
        if not actual_tools:
            return Feedback(value=True, rationale="Correctly called no tools.")
        return Feedback(
            value=False,
            rationale=f"Expected no tool calls, but called: {actual_tools}",
        )

    # Check each expected tool was called
    missing = [t for t in expected_tools if t not in actual_tools]
    if missing:
        return Feedback(
            value=False,
            rationale=f"Missing tools: {missing}. Called: {actual_tools}",
        )

    return Feedback(value=True, rationale=f"All expected tools called: {actual_tools}")


@scorer
def groundedness(inputs, outputs, trace):
    """Response numbers should come from tool output, not be hallucinated."""
    tool_outputs = get_tool_outputs(trace)

    if not tool_outputs:
        # No tools called - nothing to ground against
        return Feedback(value=True, rationale="No tool calls to check against.")

    # Collect all numbers from tool outputs
    tool_numbers = set()
    for output in tool_outputs:
        tool_numbers.update(extract_numbers(output))

    # Extract numbers from the agent's response
    response_numbers = extract_numbers(str(outputs))

    if not response_numbers:
        return Feedback(value=True, rationale="No numbers in response to verify.")

    # Check how many response numbers are grounded in tool output
    grounded = response_numbers & tool_numbers

    if not grounded:
        return Feedback(
            value=False,
            rationale="No response numbers found in tool output - possible hallucination.",
        )

    return Feedback(
        value=True,
        rationale=f"{len(grounded)} of {len(response_numbers)} response numbers grounded in tool output.",
    )


@scorer
def scope_guard(inputs, outputs, expectations, trace):
    """Off-topic questions should get zero tool calls and a polite decline."""
    expected_tools = expectations.get("expected_tools", [])

    # This scorer only applies to off-topic scenarios (expected_tools is empty)
    if expected_tools:
        return Feedback(value=True, rationale="Not an off-topic scenario, skipping.")

    actual_tools = get_tool_calls(trace)

    # Check 1: No tools should be called
    if actual_tools:
        return Feedback(
            value=False,
            rationale=f"Called tools for off-topic question: {actual_tools}",
        )

    # Check 2: Response should contain decline language
    response_lower = str(outputs).lower()
    decline_phrases = [
        "can only",
        "only assist",
        "only help",
        "sales",
        "cannot help",
        "not able to",
        "outside",
        "beyond my",
        "don't have",
        "do not have",
    ]

    if any(phrase in response_lower for phrase in decline_phrases):
        return Feedback(value=True, rationale="Correctly declined off-topic question.")

    return Feedback(
        value=False,
        rationale=(
            f"No decline language found in response. "
            f"Expected phrases like 'can only assist with sales'. "
            f"Got: {str(outputs)[:200]}"
        ),
    )


@scorer
def efficiency(inputs, outputs, expectations, trace):
    """Check the agent did not loop or make excessive calls."""
    actual_tools = get_tool_calls(trace)
    max_calls = expectations.get("max_tool_calls", 4)

    # Check total tool calls are not excessive
    if len(actual_tools) > max_calls:
        return Feedback(
            value=False,
            rationale=(
                f"Too many tool calls: {len(actual_tools)} "
                f"(expected {max_calls} or fewer)"
            ),
        )

    return Feedback(
        value=True,
        rationale=f"Efficient: {len(actual_tools)} tool call(s) within limit of {max_calls}.",
    )


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# LLM-as-Judge scorers (use the same SAP AI Core model as the agent)
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------

from first_react_agent.client import create_llm
from first_react_agent.config import AICoreConfig

# Create a separate LLM instance for judging
_judge_config = AICoreConfig()
_judge_llm = create_llm(_judge_config)


def _llm_judge(question: str, response: str, guidelines: list[str]) -> Feedback:
    """Call the LLM to judge a response against guidelines."""
    rules = "\n".join(f"- {g}" for g in guidelines)
    prompt = (
        "You are an evaluation judge for a sales data assistant. "
        "The agent can ONLY answer questions about sales data. "
        "For any off-topic question, the agent is expected to politely decline and explain what it can help with. "
        "A polite decline for an off-topic question is CORRECT behavior.\n\n"
        "Determine if the response meets all applicable guidelines.\n\n"
        f"Guidelines:\n{rules}\n\n"
        f"User question: {question}\n"
        f"Agent response: {response}\n\n"
        "Answer YES if the response meets all applicable guidelines, or NO if it does not. "
        "Then explain your reasoning in one sentence.\n\n"
        "Format: YES/NO: <rationale>"
    )
    result = _judge_llm.invoke(prompt)
    answer = result.content.strip()
    passed = answer.upper().startswith("YES")
    return Feedback(value=passed, rationale=answer)


@scorer
def clarity(inputs, outputs):
    """LLM judge: is the response clear and well-structured?"""
    return _llm_judge(
        question=inputs["question"],
        response=str(outputs),
        guidelines=[
            "The response should be well-structured and easy to read.",
            "If the response includes numbers, they should be clearly presented, not buried in long paragraphs.",
            "If the agent declines a question, the decline should be polite and explain what it can help with.",
        ],
    )


@scorer
def completeness(inputs, outputs):
    """LLM judge: does the response fully address the question?"""
    return _llm_judge(
        question=inputs["question"],
        response=str(outputs),
        guidelines=[
            "The response should directly address the user's question.",
            "If the user asks about sales data, the response should include specific numbers, not vague statements.",
            "If the question is outside the agent's scope, the response should clearly explain what it can help with instead.",
        ],
    )

eval/dataset.py (path from project root) - golden trajectory definitions:

"""Golden trajectory dataset for the sales agent."""

SALES_AGENT_DATASET = [
    # S01: Basic query - fetch and summarize
    {
        "inputs": {"question": "How were sales in January?"},
        "expectations": {
            "expected_tools": ["get_monthly_sales", "calculate_sales_summary"],
            "max_tool_calls": 4,
        },
    },
    # S02: Explicit summary request
    {
        "inputs": {"question": "Give me a complete summary of March sales"},
        "expectations": {
            "expected_tools": ["get_monthly_sales", "calculate_sales_summary"],
            "max_tool_calls": 4,
        },
    },
    # S03: Specific month and year
    {
        "inputs": {"question": "What were sales in February 2025?"},
        "expectations": {
            "expected_tools": ["get_monthly_sales", "calculate_sales_summary"],
            "max_tool_calls": 4,
        },
    },
    # S04: Off-topic - weather
    {
        "inputs": {"question": "What's the weather in Berlin?"},
        "expectations": {
            "expected_tools": [],
            "max_tool_calls": 0,
        },
    },
    # S05: Off-topic - creative
    {
        "inputs": {"question": "Write me a poem about cats"},
        "expectations": {
            "expected_tools": [],
            "max_tool_calls": 0,
        },
    },
    # S06: Multi-month comparison
    {
        "inputs": {"question": "Compare January and February sales"},
        "expectations": {
            "expected_tools": ["get_monthly_sales", "calculate_sales_summary"],
            "max_tool_calls": 6,
        },
    },
]

eval/evaluate.py (path from project root) - the main runner. This is the final version with all scorers:

"""Run evaluation suite for the sales agent."""

import os
os.environ["MLFLOW_ENABLED"] = "false"

import mlflow
from uuid import uuid4

from first_react_agent.config import AICoreConfig
from first_react_agent.agent import create_app
from eval.scorers import tool_selection, groundedness, scope_guard, efficiency
from eval.scorers import clarity, completeness  # LLM judge scorers
from eval.dataset import SALES_AGENT_DATASET

# Setup: point MLflow to the same server used for tracing
config = AICoreConfig()
mlflow.set_tracking_uri(config.mlflow_tracking_uri)
mlflow.set_experiment(config.mlflow_experiment_name)

agent = create_app()

# Fix tracing for evaluate()'s thread pool (see Step 4 explanation)
mlflow.langchain.autolog(log_traces=True, run_tracer_inline=True)
mlflow.langchain.autolog(disable=True)


def predict_fn(question: str) -> str:
    """Run the agent and return the final response text."""
    result = agent.invoke(
        {"messages": [{"role": "user", "content": question}]},
        {"configurable": {"thread_id": str(uuid4())}},
    )
    return result["messages"][-1].content


def main():
    results = mlflow.genai.evaluate(
        data=SALES_AGENT_DATASET,
        predict_fn=predict_fn,
        scorers=[
            # Deterministic (fast, reliable, free)
            tool_selection,
            groundedness,
            scope_guard,
            efficiency,
            # LLM-as-Judge (slower, costs tokens, subjective)
            clarity,
            completeness,
        ],
    )
    print(results.tables["eval_results"])


if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()

Add the eval entry point to pyproject.toml (this file is in the project root). Add the evaluate line to the existing [project.scripts] section, and add the [tool.hatch.build.targets.wheel] section so hatchling includes the eval/ package when building (by default it only discovers packages under src/) :

[project.scripts]
agent = "first_react_agent.main:main"
server = "first_react_agent.server:start"
evaluate = "eval.evaluate:main"

[tool.hatch.build.targets.wheel]
packages = ["src/first_react_agent", "eval"]

Run the complete evaluation:

Make sure your MLflow server is still running in a separate terminal (from Step 3). From the project root (first-react-agent/) :

uv run evaluate

This runs the agent against every scenario in the dataset and applies all scorers. When it finishes, copy the URL from the terminal output and open it in your browser. You will see the full evaluation dashboard:

  • Golden trajectories (one per scenario in the dataset)
  • All scorers (deterministic + LLM judges)
  • Pass/fail with rationale for each
  • Click any row to inspect the full trace (every LLM call, tool call, inputs, outputs)

This is your agent's quality gate. Run it before every deployment. If a scenario fails, investigate the trace in the MLflow UI to understand exactly what went wrong.

What you have built: A complete evaluation pipeline for an AI agent. Four deterministic scorers check that the agent calls the right tools, reports real numbers, stays in scope, and does not loop. Two LLM judges check the subjective quality of responses. Six golden trajectories cover happy paths, edge cases, and off-topic scenarios. All results are tracked in MLflow with full traces so you can debug any failure.

This same pattern applies to any agent you build - not just our sales agent. Change the tools, change the dataset, add domain-specific scorers, and you have an evaluation suite for your next project.

Final Project Structure

first-react-agent/
├── src/first_react_agent/
│   ├── __init__.py
│   ├── agent.py           # + MLflow tracing setup (autolog, tracking URI, experiment)
│   ├── client.py          # LLM client (unchanged)
│   ├── config.py          # + MLflow settings (mlflow_enabled, tracking_uri, experiment_name)
│   ├── tools.py           # get_monthly_sales, calculate_sales_summary (unchanged)
│   ├── schemas.py         # Pydantic models (unchanged)
│   ├── server.py          # FastAPI server (unchanged, from Blog 3)
│   └── main.py            # CLI entry point (unchanged)
├── ui/                    # SAPUI5 chat frontend (unchanged, from Blog 3)
│   ├── package.json
│   ├── ui5.yaml
│   └── webapp/
│       ├── index.html
│       ├── manifest.json
│       ├── Component.js
│       ├── controller/
│       │   └── App.controller.js
│       ├── view/
│       │   └── App.view.xml
│       └── css/
│           └── style.css
├── eval/
│   ├── __init__.py
│   ├── scorers.py         # 6 scorers: 4 deterministic + 2 LLM judges (clarity, completeness)
│   ├── dataset.py         # 6 golden trajectories (S01-S06)
│   └── evaluate.py        # Main runner: agent + scorers + dataset + 2 LLM judges
├── pyproject.toml         # + mlflow[genai] dependency + evaluate entry point
├── sales.csv
└── .env                   # + MLFLOW_ENABLED, MLFLOW_TRACKING_URI, MLFLOW_EXPERIMENT_NAME

What changed from Blog 3:

File Change

.envAdded MLFLOW_ENABLED, MLFLOW_TRACKING_URI, MLFLOW_EXPERIMENT_NAME
config.pyAdded mlflow_enabled, mlflow_tracking_uri, mlflow_experiment_name fields
agent.pyAdded MLflow tracing setup inside create_app() (reads from config)
pyproject.tomlAdded mlflow[genai] dependency and evaluate script entry point
eval/ directoryNew - 4 files containing the complete evaluation suite

The agent code itself is unchanged beyond the MLflow integration. Evaluation is a separate concern - it observes and checks the agent without modifying it.

What We Did Not Cover

This blog focused on pre-deployment evaluation - building a test suite and running it before you ship. MLflow's evaluation capabilities go further. Here is what is available when you are ready for the next level:

Evaluating production traces (no re-run needed):
Once your agent is live, you do not need to re-run it to evaluate. MLflow stores every trace from production. You can pull traces with mlflow.search_traces() and run your scorers against them:

import mlflow

# Pull the last 50 traces from production
traces = mlflow.search_traces(
    experiment_ids=["1"],
    max_results=50,
)

# Run scorers against existing traces (no predict_fn needed)
results = mlflow.genai.evaluate(
    data=traces,
    scorers=[tool_selection, groundedness],
)

This is how the "Production (nightly)" row from our deployment table works in practice. You schedule a nightly job that pulls recent traces and evaluates them. No need to re-run the agent - you evaluate what actually happened in production. See Evaluating Traces for details.

Automatic online evaluation (continuous monitoring):
For real-time monitoring, MLflow can automatically evaluate traces as they are logged - no scheduled jobs needed. You register a scorer and start it:

from mlflow.genai.scorers import Guidelines

clarity = Guidelines(
    name="clarity",
    guidelines=["The response should be well-structured and easy to read."],
)

# Register the scorer and start automatic evaluation
scorer_version = clarity.register()
scorer_version.start(sample_rate=0.1)  # Evaluate 10% of traces

Every trace that matches your filter gets evaluated automatically. Results appear in the MLflow UI alongside the trace. This is how you catch quality degradation in real time without waiting for a nightly run. See Automatic Evaluations for setup instructions.

Human feedback and ground truth:
Automated scorers catch measurable issues, but some problems only a human can spot. MLflow provides APIs to capture human feedback on production traces:

import mlflow

# A human reviewer marks a trace as incorrect
mlflow.log_feedback(
    trace_id="abc-123",
    name="human_review",
    value=False,
    rationale="Agent reported wrong month's data",
    source=mlflow.entities.AssessmentSource(
        source_type="HUMAN",
        source_id="reviewer@company.com",
    ),
)

You can also annotate traces with ground truth expectations using mlflow.log_expectation(), then build evaluation datasets from those annotated traces. This creates a feedback loop: production issues become test cases that prevent the same issue from happening again. See Feedback and Expectations for more.

Multi-turn conversation evaluation:
Our sales agent handles single questions. If your agent handles multi-turn conversations (chat), MLflow provides session-level scorers:

from mlflow.genai.scorers import (
    ConversationCompleteness,  # Did the agent resolve the user's goal?
    UserFrustration,           # Did the user get frustrated?
    KnowledgeRetention,        # Did the agent remember context?
)

You can also use ConversationSimulator to generate synthetic multi-turn conversations from test scenarios, so you do not need real users to test conversation quality. See Multi-Turn Evaluation and Conversation Simulation for details.

Comparing evaluation runs:
When you change your agent and re-run the evaluation suite, MLflow saves each run separately. You can compare runs side by side in the MLflow UI to see which scorers improved, which regressed, and by how much. This is how you track quality over time - each evaluation run becomes a data point in your agent's quality history.

Performance tip: parallel evaluation:
By default, evaluate() runs test cases one at a time. For larger datasets, set the MLFLOW_GENAI_EVAL_MAX_WORKERS environment variable to run multiple test cases in parallel:

MLFLOW_GENAI_EVAL_MAX_WORKERS=4 uv run evaluate

This runs 4 test cases concurrently, which can significantly speed up evaluation for larger datasets.

What's Next?

Blog 5: Multi-Agent Systems?

entry.title: Re: "Missing connection of material in plant to the mater...
entry.content: Hello Chrismugabo, Did you fix this error message? I am a beginner that I started my project on a sandbox from scratch. Recently I came across the same problem, it is a prerequisite for Goods Receipts via MIGO. I would like to share my solution: 1. Verify in MM03, ML Act. is not active 2. Verify via T-Code: OMX1 if ML Act is active for the Valuation Area/Plant and Company Code. 3. To execute program Material and Plant (Valuation area) ML_REPAIR_ML_MASTERDATA via T-Code: SE38 4. Verify the “Accounting1” view if the ML Act is active and the Price Determ. has the value that indicated into Activation of Material Ledger (T-Code: OMX1) via T-Code: MM03. I hope it might help.

entry.title: SAP RDP: ES Plastic Tax - Missing Tax ID for delivieries in EU Acquisition Book
entry.content:
Dear SAP Responsible Design and Production Team,

I have a customer that is missing some information about tax ID and full name in the EU Acquisition Inventory Book for the Spanish Plastic Tax report output. Tax ID is displayed for goods receipts, but missing for deliveries for the same supplier. The deliveries go from a German warehouse to a Spanish plant or directly to their Spanish customers. My customer then expects the VAT number of their German warehouse to be displayed in the EU Inventory Book.

Are there some known issues connected to deliveries or are only material documents expected? Thank you very much.

Best regards,

Jessy

entry.title: Re: How to use Inspection type 1708 in QM-EWM
entry.content:
https://help.sap.com/docs/SUPPORT_CONTENT/sewm/4248185216.html?locale=en-US

The Idea here is we need to create a new document type in EWM and S4 for those Stock transfer.
This document type is mapped in /n/scwm/qrsetup to 1708 inspection type.
So after doing Stock transfer movement in S4 it will trigger a inbound delivery to EWM.
Later system will pick the inspection type from there and create a inspection lot. 

Direct stock posting in EWM via /n/scwm/post will not create any inspection lot.

entry.title: Re: SAP RDP: ES Plastic Tax - Missing Tax ID for delivier...
entry.content:
The Spanish Plastic Tax report uses material documents (such as goods receipts and goods issues) as its source of information. These documents carry supplier tax IDs, which is why the VAT number is shown correctly for goods receipts.

For deliveries, however, the system does not always include tax ID fields for the supplying warehouse or plant. This is standard system behavior, not a defect. The report is designed to reflect acquisitions from suppliers, not internal plant transfers. As a result, the VAT number of the German warehouse is not automatically displayed in the EU Inventory Book.

Options to address this:

  • Ensure that VAT registration numbers are correctly maintained in the master data of the supplying plant/company.
  • If business requires the VAT number of the German warehouse to appear, a small enhancement may be needed to enrich the report with plant VAT information during delivery postings.

Thank you in advance for any insights you can share.

entry.title: Your Cloud Future Starts Here: Core Capabilities of SAP BTP Explained
entry.content:
A New Expert Series for Architects, Developers, and IT Leaders

The cloud journey isn’t a single leap. It’s more like assembling a high-performance engine while it’s already running.

With SAP Business Technology Platform (SAP BTP) at the center, organizations are not just moving to the cloud—they’re building intelligent, integrated, and future-ready landscapes. But to truly unlock its value, understanding the core capabilities is essential.

SAP Business Technology Platform (SAP BTP) continues to evolve at a rapid pace—bringing new capabilities for integration, automation, security, governance, and application development. With so much innovation happening across the platform, staying up to date can feel like a full‑time job.

To help you navigate this landscape, we’re launching a new expert-led series:

 Your Cloud Future Starts Here: Core Capabilities of SAP BTP Explained

Across multiple deep-dive sessions, SAP product managers and engineering experts will walk you through the latest capabilities, best practices, and strategic direction of SAP BTP. Whether you’re modernizing landscapes, building cloud-native extensions, or strengthening your security posture, this series gives you the insights you need to succeed.

Each session is live, free, and open to the entire SAP Community—with demos, roadmaps, and the opportunity to learn directly from the teams building SAP BTP.

Below is your complete overview of all upcoming sessions.

 

Apr 21 — Govern your cloud deliveries with SAP Cloud ALM and SAP Cloud Transport Management

 

Learn about the latest innovations in SAP Cloud ALM and SAP Cloud Transport Management Service, including modifiable transport requests in SAP BTP. See how real-time adjustments streamline change management across cloud landscapes.

Speakers:

Sven Rullmann, Product Manager SAP Cloud Transport Management Service
Moritz Gysler, Product Manager SAP Cloud ALM

Date & time: April 21, 2026 at 15:00 CEST / 9 AM EDT / 18:30 IST

  • Registration: https://url.sap/4593sn
  • YouTube Live: https://youtube.com/live/cSu2b8WgaWw
  • Add to calendar: https://community.sap.com/khhcw49343/downloadCalendar?message.id=14359708

 

Apr 28 — SAP Cloud ALM for SAP BTP Operations: Insights and Strategy Update

 

 

Get an overview of how SAP Cloud ALM supports SAP BTP operations, plus strategy updates and a look at the roadmap.

Speakers:

Stephanie Jerch, Product Manager SAP Cloud ALM for Operations
Carsten Heinz, Chief Product Owner SAP Cloud ALM for Operations

Date & time: April 28, 2026 at 15:00 CEST / 9 AM EDT / 18:30 IST

  • Registration: https://url.sap/4593sn
  • YouTube Live: https://youtube.com/live/9SDA6FPbufI
  • Add to calendar: https://community.sap.com/khhcw49343/downloadCalendar?message.id=14359710

 

Jun 18 — From AI-Driven Insights to Automated Operations with SAP Automation Pilot and SAP Cloud ALM

 

See how SAP Automation Pilot and SAP AI Core work together to collect, analyze, and act on operational data—enabling a true closed-loop operations model.

Speaker:

Biser Simeonov, Product Manager, SAP

Date & time: June 18, 2026 at15:00 CEST / 9 AM EDT / 18:30 IST

entry.title: Re: Project stock transfer SLOC to SLOC - how to reduce f...
entry.content:
Dear Customer,

the easiest way would be to use Fiori app Transfer Stock - in plant (F1061). Simply select the Project Stock (Q) in in Source Storage location, select the target Storage Location and in the subsequent pop up enter the quantity rsp adjust the WBS Element in case more than one Special Stock Type Project Stock with different WBS element exists (See attachment).

Result is a material document transfer posting (see attachment).

When using Fiori F1061 you do not have to worry about movement types, stock location and stock type. The app guides you to achieve the desired result intuitively.

Best regards

Bernd

entry.title: Re: Migration Objects listed in Help Portal but not avail...
entry.content:
Hello,

Thank you for bringing up this migration object visibility issue. I can confirm that all 9 Direct Transfer migration objects you’ve listed should indeed be available in S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition systems:

  1. Business partner (Direct Transfer)
  2. MM - Purchasing info record (Direct Transfer)
  3. Object classification (general template) (Direct Transfer)
  4. PP - Material BOM (Direct Transfer)
  5. PP - Production order (Direct Transfer)
  6. PP - Production version (Direct Transfer)
  7. PP - Routing (Direct Transfer)
  8. PP - Work center (Direct Transfer)
  9. Pricing condition (sales) (Direct Transfer)

Since these objects are documented as supported and should be accessible via Fiori App F8638 (Migration Object Search), but are not appearing in your system, this appears to be a system-specific issue rather than expected behavior.

Recommendation:

Please raise a support ticket with SAP (component: CA-DT-MIG-S4C) to investigate why these Direct Transfer migration objects are not visible in your system. When creating the ticket, include:

• Your system name, client number, and product version
• Screenshots of the F8638 search results showing the missing objects
• Reference to the Help Portal documentation URL you provided
• Complete list of the missing objects

The Direct Transfer approach is not deprecated for Public Edition, and you should have access to these migration objects as documented.

Best regards

entry.title: Cumulative Calculation Across Blended Models in SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC)
entry.content:
Hi all,
I am working with three models in SAP Analytics Cloud: Sales, Comp Stock, and Purchase. Each model contains a YearMonth dimension, and I have linked these models in a story using the Link Dimensions (Blending) option. Additionally, Material ID is also used as a linking dimension.

Requirement:
I need to calculate cumulative values (e.g., Quantity, Sales, Cost, Comp Stock) based on the selected YearMonth:

  • If Dec 2025 is selected → cumulative should be from Jan 2025 to Dec 2025
  • If Jan 2026 is selected → cumulative should be from Feb 2025 to Jan 2026 (rolling 12 months)
  • If no YearMonth is selected → it should default to cumulative up to the current month

Issue:
I have tried the following approaches, but none are working correctly due to model blending:

  • Running Total
  • Restricted Measures
  • Aggregation Calculations

These either fail to respect the blended model context or do not compute correctly across models.

Question:
What is the recommended approach to calculate cumulative (especially rolling 12-month) values across blended models in SAC?
Is there a way to handle this via calculated measures, advanced formulas, or model-level changes?

Any guidance or best practices would be greatly appreciated.

entry.title: Re: Excess Quantity Not Allowed For Production Order
entry.content: In Public Cloud, you cannot confirm more than the planned quantity. To resolve the error, increase the production order quantity (not just the component requirement) so that confirmations remain consistent. This is the only standard way to handle excess quantity confirmations in Public Cloud.

entry.title: Re: SAP Datasphere: Extract, Transform & Load (ETL) - Best Practices
entry.content:
Hi Jeet,

Thank you for your contribution. I had been looking for an article like this for quite a while, especially one that provides such a clear and comprehensive overview of the different features available for ETL Flows.

Based on my experience with SAP Datasphere so far, I would like to share a few considerations on some of the points raised in your article.

I have always used Data Flow instead of Replication Flow or Transformation Flow. The reason is that Data Flow allows me to load data processed by a view created in Datasphere into a Local Table. I tried to build the same pipeline using Replication Flow, but I found that it is not possible to select Datasphere local repository objects as source and target in the same flow.

Of course, I may be overlooking something, but if that is indeed the case, then Data Flow currently seems to be the only feature that makes it possible to set up a periodic historization process for query result sets by appending them to a Local Table.

Another observation concerns the comparison between Replication Flow and Remote Table Replication.

I am struggling to understand how these two objects can really be compared. When we talk about Remote Tables, this feature is already natively available in the Data Builder and can then be managed and monitored through the Data Integration Monitor. For this reason, I do not really see the benefit of creating and configuring an additional artifact, such as a Replication Flow, to perform what appears to be the same task.

This is just my point of view, and I would be very interested to hear your perspective on it.

Thank you again.

entry.title: Avoid Using a Slash in Product Codes in SAP Commerce Cloud
entry.content:
In SAP Commerce Cloud, using a forward slash (/) in Product or GenericVariantProduct codes can lead to unexpected issues, such as broken product pages and failed search results. This behavior is not a defect; rather, it is a consequence of standard web protocols and the underlying architecture of SAP Commerce Cloud. 

The following sections explain why we recommend avoiding the use of slashes in product codes.


1. How URLs Are Processed (Apache Is the First Barrier)


When a user clicks a product link, the request flow is:

Browser → Apache Web Server → Tomcat/Spring → Commerce components (Solr, search services, etc.)

Apache processes requests before they reach SAP Commerce. By default, the Apache directive AllowEncodedSlashes is set to Off, which means:

Forward slashes (/), backslashes (\), encoded slashes (%2F), or encoded backslashes (%5C) are rejected at the web server level
Requests containing these characters return a 404 (Not Found)
The request never reaches Tomcat, Spring, or any Commerce logic
This behavior is intentional and serves as an important security measure.

While Apache can technically bypass this restriction by setting:

AllowEncodedSlashes NoDecode

 
this only addresses the web server layer and introduces security considerations. More importantly:

  • It does not resolve downstream issues in SAP Commerce
  • It does not change how URLs are interpreted structurally
  • It does not prevent inconsistencies in Solr, routing, or application logic

Framework-level logic (Spring MVC, filters, controllers) executes only after Apache forwards the request, so relying on Apache configuration is neither complete nor recommended.

2. PDP and OCC Endpoints Depend on Path-Based URLs

SAP Commerce PDP (Product Detail Page) and OCC endpoints are GET-based and rely on a path-based URL structure:

/{productCode}
/products/{code}


 If an encoded product code (e.g., ABC%2F123) reaches Commerce, customization is required to handle it properly. When implementing such customizations:

  • Encoding and decoding must be consistently managed at every layer
  • Inconsistent handling can cause subtle errors, data inconsistencies, or unpredictable platform behavior


Alternative approaches, such as using a query parameter (?productCode=ABC/123) or a POST body, may bypass this limitation. However, they are not recommended because they:

  • Require significant architectural changes
  • Disrupt SEO and caching behavior
  • Impact standard out-of-the-box (OOTB) link generation and OCC contracts

Even workarounds like converting ABC/123 → ABC-123 introduce:

  • Custom URL mapping
  • Bidirectional mapping logic (URL ↔ original code)
  • Risk of identifier collisions
  • Ongoing upgrade and maintenance overhead

These approaches deviate from standard SAP Commerce design and are not OOTB supported.

3. Solr Search Is Also Affected


Using slashes in product codes can create search and retrieval issues:

  • Search query mismatch: Queries must be properly encoded/decoded to match indexed values
  • Indexing vs. retrieval inconsistencies: Product codes may not resolve correctly across search, PDP, or APIs
  • Faceting and filtering issues: Slashes can break filter queries or require additional escaping logic

These issues occur within the Commerce application layer, so Apache changes alone cannot resolve them.

4. Data Consistency and Debugging Complexity


Since the issue spans multiple layers:

  • Apache (URL rejection)
  • Commerce routing (path-based PDP)
  • Solr (index/query parsing)

It can lead to hard-to-debug inconsistencies, where the same product behaves differently across:

  • Search results
  • PDP rendering
  • OCC APIs


5. Recommended Approach


To ensure long-term stability and supportability:

Use URL-safe product codes (avoid /, \, %2F, %5C)
If business identifiers require special characters, introduce a URL-safe surrogate key for storefront exposure
Avoid relying on Apache configuration changes as a workaround
Following these practices ensures consistent, reliable, and supportable behavior across SAP Commerce Cloud.

entry.title: Re: Asset Report not executed in ECC
entry.content:
Hi @Reshan_Ranasinghe ,
My friend you are seeing this error because SAP Asset Accounting requires a technical fiscal year change (AJRW) to be executed for the company code—even if the fiscal year/periods appear open in OAAQ. Opening periods in OAAQ alone is not enough; the system must carry forward asset values into the new fiscal year before asset reports can run.

Please follow the steps mentioned below
1. Go to Transaction  code AJRW
2. Enter Company Code XXX
3. Enter the New Fiscal Year (e.g., 2026)

Run in background (SAP recommends this for performance). Check the log for errors.

Please accept my solution if you resolve the issue by using my solution.

Regards

Re: Need All Characters in Uppercase in DMEE Payment File...
entry.content:
Hi @vijayhiremath ,
My friend In a DMEE tree, the correct way to force all alphanumeric characters to uppercase is to use the standard conversion rule “All characters in upper case (capital letters)”, which is available under Character String conversion rules.

SAP DMEE supports several built‑in conversion rules for character strings. One of them is explicitly designed for your requirement. Conversion Rule to Use is “All characters in upper case (capital letters)”
This is the correct conversion function for uppercase transformation in DMEE.
It is part of the standard conversion options for Character String fields.

Hope above solution will help you. Please accept is it helped you.

entry.title: Release of System-Based Certification for SAP Service Cloud Version 2
entry.content:
Hello everyone, 

On behalf of the product learning team, we are pleased to announce the updated and first of its kind system based certification for SAP Service Cloud Version 2. 

SAP Certified - Implementation Consultant - SAP Service Cloud Version 2

This milestone marks a major shift in how SAP validates real-world skills by moving away from traditional theory-based assessments and toward practical, scenario-driven, hands-on evaluations.

 

🎯Who Is This Certification For?

This certification is designed for:
  • Implementation consultants
  • System administrators

🌟A New Era of Practical, System-Based Assessment

Unlike standard multiple-choice exams, this new certification provides candidates with:
  • A live SAP Service Cloud Version 2 system, and
  • A PDF containing a set of tasks to complete
Candidates perform actual configuration and scenario-based exercises directly in the system. Once all required tasks are completed, they can submit their results for automated evaluation. Within minutes, candidates will know whether they have passed or failed — offering a faster, more authentic, and more meaningful way to validate capability.
This practical approach ensures that certified consultants are not only knowledgeable but also skilled in applying SAP Service Cloud Version 2 functionality in realistic customer scenarios.
 

🎯Why System Based Certifications?

The reimagined SAP Certification exams offer a range of benefits. More specifically:

  • Job-ready proof: Demonstrates you can navigate SAP Service Cloud Version 2 and execute business-critical tasks—not just recall concepts.
  • Strong SAP signal: An SAP-recognised credential and digital badge that hiring managers and project leads across the ecosystem trust.
  • Practice over memorisation: Preparation aligns to SAP Learning Journeys and hands-on exercises for better retention and impact.
  • Always current: Content and recertification align with SAP’s cloud release cycle, reflecting the latest features and UI. 
  • Higher integrity than Q&A-only: Performance on practical tasks reduces guesswork and emphasises authentic ability.

📚How to Prepare

To set yourself up for success, we recommend the following preparation path:

1. Complete the SAP Service Cloud Version 2 Learning Courses

The new learning journey is available on learning.sap.com provide the essential and implementation knowledge you need before attempting the hands‑on tasks. 

2. Practice Using the Hands-On System Exercises

Strengthen your confidence by trying out interactive exercises on a practical system . This helps you become familiar with the interfaces, workflows, and configuration steps you’ll be expected to perform during the certification. 

 

📢Spread the Word!

Help us share this exciting update with partners, colleagues, and other stakeholders.

📝We Value Your Feedback

After completing the certification, please take a moment to share your feedback. Your insights help us continuously improve the learning experience.

entry.title: Re: Bypassing Commitment Item Check for Certain Transactions
entry.content:
Hi @Lael_K ,
My friend it look like you have already done the right checks — the commitment item is correctly derived from the GL account, and FMDERIVE shows the correct result. When FBCJ still throws “No commitment item entered in item 00001 1001”, it usually means the issue is not with derivation itself, but with how the cash journal posting interacts with Funds Management (FM) settings.

Below are the most common root causes and how to fix them. I’ll walk you through them clearly so you can pinpoint the exact one in your system.

1. If Funds Management is active for the company code, SAP will check commitment items for all postings — including cash journal — unless you explicitly exempt them.
Goto SPRO → Public Sector Management → Funds Management → Basic Settings → Activate/Deactivate FM for Applications
Check whether Cash Journal (FBCJ) is active for FM. If you do not want FM checks for FBCJ then
Deactivate FM for Cash Journal for the relevant company code. This immediately stops the commitment item check for FBCJ postings.

2. If the commitment item is derived, SAP may reject it if the financial transaction (FT) on the GL account is incompatible and the commitment item category does not match the posting type. Check In FMGLFLEX or FMDERIVE, verify, Financial transaction (e.g., 30, 40, 50). Commitment item category (e.g., 1 = revenue, 2 = expense, 3 = statistical). Similar issues when Cash journal postings often use financial transaction 30 (Expenditure). If your commitment item is Revenue or Statistical, SAP rejects it.

If you cannot fix with above suggestions you can message me to give you some more suggestions.

entry.title: Re: How to post negative sales in S4
entry.content: In S/4HANA, negative sales from POS should be routed to a special storage location. When stock arrives, the system transfers from the main location to this special location to clear the negative balance. This is the recommended way to handle POS negative sales without enabling negative stock in the main inventory.
